title: Furstenberg Entropy Spectrum of Stationary Actions
speaker: Tianyi Zheng
abstract: In this talk we will discuss some aspects of the question: given a group, what is the range of Furstenberg entropy of ergodic stationary actions of it? For the special linear group and its lattices, constraints on this spectrum come from Nevo-Zimmer structure theorems; and entropy values can be realized based on Poisson bundles over stationary random subgroups. The complete description is not yet known for other simple Lie groups. Based on joint work with Jeremie Brieussel.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Stationary actions and Furstenberg entropy spectrum
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a locally compact group G and random-walk law mu, defines mu-stationary actions and Furstenberg entropy via KL divergence, and frames the spectrum as entropy values of ergodic stationary systems lying between 0 and the Poisson-boundary entropy.

proof_stages.label: Bowen's free-group full-interval template
proof_stages.summary: Bowen's theorem for the free group with uniform generator measure gives the whole entropy interval using Poisson bundles over invariant random subgroups, providing the flexibility model for later interpolation.

proof_stages.label: Nevo-Zimmer projective-factor constraints
proof_stages.summary: For higher-rank semisimple groups with admissible mu, the projective factor theorem forces every non-measure-preserving stationary system to factor onto some G/Q, and under the relative measure-preserving hypothesis only the finitely many parabolic entropy values occur.

proof_stages.label: SL_d parabolic splitting and spectrum theorem
proof_stages.summary: For G=SL_d(R), the parabolic Q is modified to Q-hat by splitting each 2x2 diagonal block; systems with maximal projective factor G/Q fill the entropy interval from h(G/Q) to h(G/Q-hat), and the full spectrum is the union of these intervals over parabolics.

proof_stages.label: Scope, rank-one blocks, and entropy gaps
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the method relies on having a free-group lattice in the relevant SL_2 block, and that rank-one property-T simple groups point to entropy gaps not explained by the Nevo-Zimmer projective-factor theorem.

proof_stages.label: Stationary random subgroups from induced F2 actions
proof_stages.summary: Since higher-rank groups do not have enough invariant random subgroups, the proof uses stationary random subgroups, produced by inducing an F2 measure-preserving action through the SL_2 block, through Q, and then up to G, and taking stabilizers.

proof_stages.label: Stationary Poisson bundles and entropy interpolation
proof_stages.summary: For non-invariant subgroup laws the product construction is replaced by a stationary joining built from the boundary map and Doob transform; quotienting trajectories by the subgroup gives a Poisson bundle whose entropy depends continuously on the input F2 action, yielding the interval interpolation.

examples.name: Ends of the free group F2
examples.note: The boundary of reduced infinite words with its uniform hitting measure is used as an easy stationary action, illustrating that natural stationary measures can exist even when invariant measures do not.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker names the ends/boundary of F2 as a basic stationary action with harmonic measure but does not actually verify stationarity or compute anything on it.

passes.reason: The end boundary is the Poisson boundary for the simple random walk, hence maximal in entropy, and is used to show stationarity need not imply an invariant probability measure.
The boundary/ends of F2 is used as a standard stationary-action example rather than to refute a claim.
the boundary/ends of F2 are used as a standard stationary-action example, not as an adversarial case, and are structurally typical here
The F2 end boundary is the Poisson boundary/maximal-entropy stationary action and is used to show stationarity can occur without an invariant measure.
The ends of F2 form the highly regular regular-tree Poisson boundary, which is entropy-maximal for the walk and is used to exhibit a stationary action with no invariant measure.
For the simple walk the end boundary is the Poisson boundary/maximal stationary boundary, used to witness stationarity without an invariant measure.
The ends of F2 with the uniform harmonic measure form the regular-tree Poisson boundary, hence a maximal-entropy and highly homogeneous stationary action used as a basic illustration.
The ends of F2 are used as the basic stationary/Poisson-boundary action, which is maximal in Furstenberg entropy rather than a refutation.
Used as a basic stationary action, and for the simple random walk it is the regular-tree Poisson boundary, hence the maximal-entropy boundary object.
The end boundary of the regular Cayley tree of F2 is used as the Poisson/stationary boundary with maximal boundary entropy, not as an obstruction.
Used to exhibit a stationary action with no invariant measure; structurally it is the regular-tree Poisson boundary, hence symmetric and entropy-maximal.
Used to show a stationary action need not have an invariant measure; structurally it is the regular-tree Poisson boundary, hence maximal for Furstenberg entropy.

examples.name: Splitting a 2x2 block in an SL_d parabolic
examples.note: A concrete block upper-triangular parabolic is used to make the operation Q -> Q-hat visible by inserting an intermediate flag inside each two-dimensional block.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly runs the q-to-qhat construction on SL_d parabolics by identifying 2x2 diagonal blocks and inserting an intermediate flag dimension, with concrete block examples.

passes.reason: A 2x2 Levi block is the minimal nontrivial rank-one SL2 factor whose splitting is used constructively to interpolate entropy values.
A 2x2 Levi block is the minimal rank-one block whose splitting gives the critical parabolic refinement used for interpolation.
a 2x2 parabolic block is the minimal rank-one Levi case where the SL2 flexibility appears, and it is used constructively rather than to refute a claim
A 2x2 block is the critical minimal rank-one SL2 Levi factor, and splitting it gives the endpoint parabolic Q-hat in the entropy interval construction.
A 2x2 block is the minimal nontrivial rank-one Levi block whose splitting gives a boundary/minimal-parabolic refinement, used constructively to interpolate entropy values.
A 2x2 Levi block is the critical rank-one SL2 case whose splitting defines Q-hat and enables interpolation, not a refutation.
A 2x2 block is the minimal nontrivial rank-one SL2 Levi block, and splitting it gives the endpoint parabolic used for interpolation rather than a refutation.
A 2x2 block is the minimal nontrivial rank-one Levi factor, and splitting it gives the boundary refinement used for the entropy interval construction.
Used to define the SL_d parabolic refinement; the 2x2 block is the minimal nontrivial/rank-one Levi block where the flexibility occurs.
A 2x2 diagonal block is the minimal nontrivial/rank-one block whose splitting gives the critical parabolic refinement used for interpolation.
The split 2x2 block is the minimal rank-one SL2 Levi case that gives an interpolation endpoint, not a refutation.
Used constructively to define the interval endpoints; the 2x2 block is the minimal nontrivial/rank-one Levi case and its split is a boundary refinement.

examples.name: Rank-one property-T groups Sp(n,1) and F4(-20)
examples.note: These rank-one simple Lie groups show that zero can be isolated in the entropy spectrum even where the higher-rank projective-factor obstruction is absent, motivating the search for additional constraints.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker cites Sp(n,1) and F4(-20) as rank-one property-T examples and states consequences/questions about entropy gaps without doing a calculation or construction on them.

passes.reason: These are rank-one minimal-rank simple Lie groups, with F4(-20) exceptional, used to show property T creates an entropy gap at zero obstructing full-interval spectra.
These are rank-one minimal-rank simple Lie groups used as property-T obstructions to full interval flexibility, with F4(-20) an exceptional Lie-group case.
these are minimal real-rank simple groups giving a property-T entropy gap that obstructs full-interval flexibility, with F4(-20) an exceptional Lie-group case
These are minimal-rank simple Lie groups with property-T entropy gaps, including the exceptional real form F4(-20), used as obstructions to rank-one flexibility/full intervals.
These are real-rank-one edge cases with property T, including the exceptional F4(-20), used as an obstruction showing a zero entropy gap not explained by projective-factor constraints.
These are real-rank-one simple groups at the minimal-rank boundary, with F4(-20) exceptional, used to show a property-T entropy gap beyond projective-factor constraints.
These rank-one property-T simple groups are invoked as an obstruction to full interval flexibility via an entropy gap at zero, with rank one minimal and F4(-20) an exceptional Lie-group real form.
These rank-one property-T groups obstruct full interval flexibility by making zero isolated, with rank one minimal and F4(-20) an exceptional Lie-group case.
Invoked as rank-one property-T obstructions to full interval flexibility, with minimal real rank and including the exceptional Lie group F4(-20).
These rank-one property-T groups are invoked as obstructions producing a gap at zero beyond projective-factor constraints, with F4(-20) an exceptional Lie real form.
They obstruct full rank-one entropy flexibility via property-T gaps; they are rank-one symmetric-space groups, with F4(-20) exceptional.
Used as rank-one property-T cases where zero is isolated, obstructing full-interval flexibility; rank one is minimal real rank and F4(-20) is exceptional.
